coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent sensor based on triazole-pyridine derivative for selective detection of mercury (II) ions in different real water samples: Experimental and DFT calculations
coredata.dc:creator: Elbayoumy, Elsayed
Elhendawy, Mohamed
Gaafar, Mostafa M.
Moawed, EA
aboelnga, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Toxic heavy metals, particularly mercury II ions, must be selectively detected in industrial effluent to restrict their percentage and thereby aid in environmental protection. The present study describes the synthesis of 6-(4-(4-(5-cyano-6-oxo-4-styryl)-1,6-dihydropyridin-2-yl)-5-methyl-1H-1,2,3-triazol-1-yl)phenyl)-2-oxo-4-styryl)-1,2-dihydropyridine-3-carbonitrile (TOHC) and its ability as a novel selective fluorescent probe for the detection of mercury II ions in different real water samples. The structure of the prepared TOHC was confirmed by both FTIR and 1HNMR analysis, while crystal structure and thermal stability was investigated using XRD and TGA techniques. The optical properties of TOHC were investigated using both UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy at different pH values. Its luminescent properties obtained when TOHC is excited by a wavelength equal to 424 nm, where a maximum emission peak has observed at a wavelength equal to 466.6 nm. Moreover, using Stern-Volmer equation indicate that there is a good correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of TOHC accompanied with high quenching rate equal to 2.24 x 104 mol−1.L. Selectivity of TOHC toward Hg2+ ions was confirmed by the assistance of fifteen different metal cations. In addition, Job’s Plot method and UV–vis spectroscopy were used to estimate the stoichiometry as well as understand the quenching mechanism between TOHC sensor and Hg2+ ions. The applicability of TOHC sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions in real samples was further validated using five different real water samples and exhibited excellent results. Lastly, DFT calculations were conducted to provide atomistic insights into the chemical interaction between Hg(II) and TOHC sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Mercury detection
Triazole-pyridine compounds
Luminescent properties
DFT calculations

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of mercury (II) ions in real water sample
Interaction mechanism between TOHC and Hg2+ ions
Detection analysis of mercury in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of AZPE, AMTP, and TOHC; (b) 1H NMR spectrum of TOHC.
(a) XRD of TOHC; (b) TGA and DTG of TOHC.
(a) UV– vis spectra of TOHC in 1,4-dioxane; (b) fluorescence spectra of TOHC in 1,4-dioxane.
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of TOHC.
Fluorescence spectra (A) and Stern-Volmer plot (B) of TOHC (2.5x 10−4 mol/L) upon adding different concentration of Hg2+ (0 – 75 mol/L) in 1,4-dioxane solvent.
The selectivity of TOHC for Hg2+ over various metal cations.
(a) Job's plot for TOHC and Hg2+ ([TOHC] + [Hg(II)]) = 5 × 10−4M at λ = 454 nm. (b) UV–visible spectra of TOHC (2.2 x 10−5 M) before and after addition 500 µM of Hg(II) ions (10−3 M).
The optimized molecular geometry of the most stable TOHC-Hg(II) together with their HOMO and LUMO.
Synthesis of AZPE, AMTP, and TOHC.
Proposed sensing mechanism between the two possible isomers, (a) enol and (b) ketone, of the chromophore TOHC and Hg(II).
Thermal activation energy and thermodynamic parameters for the degradation of TOHC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a calculated from the slope and intercept of Coats-Redfern relationship (Figure S3 in the supplementary information). b calculated according to Eqs. (3) – (5).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of Hg2+ ions detection in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent sensor based on triazole-pyridine derivative for selective detection of mercury (II) ions in different real water samples: Experimental and DFT calculations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Damietta University, New Damietta 34517, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Damietta University, New Damietta 34517, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Toxic heavy metals, particularly mercury II ions, must be selectively detected in industrial effluent to restrict their percentage and thereby aid in environmental protection. The present study describes the synthesis of 6-(4-(4-(5-cyano-6-oxo-4-styryl)-1,6-dihydropyridin-2-yl)-5-methyl-1H-1,2,3-triazol-1-yl)phenyl)-2-oxo-4-styryl)-1,2-dihydropyridine-3-carbonitrile (TOHC) and its ability as a novel selective fluorescent probe for the detection of mercury II ions in different real water samples. The structure of the prepared TOHC was confirmed by both FTIR and 1HNMR analysis, while crystal structure and thermal stability was investigated using XRD and TGA techniques. The optical properties of TOHC were investigated using both UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy at different pH values. Its luminescent properties obtained when TOHC is excited by a wavelength equal to 424 nm, where a maximum emission peak has observed at a wavelength equal to 466.6 nm. Moreover, using Stern-Volmer equation indicate that there is a good correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of TOHC accompanied with high quenching rate equal to 2.24 x 104 mol−1.L. Selectivity of TOHC toward Hg2+ ions was confirmed by the assistance of fifteen different metal cations. In addition, Job’s Plot method and UV–vis spectroscopy were used to estimate the stoichiometry as well as understand the quenching mechanism between TOHC sensor and Hg2+ ions. The applicability of TOHC sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions in real samples was further validated using five different real water samples and exhibited excellent results. Lastly, DFT calculations were conducted to provide atomistic insights into the chemical interaction between Hg(II) and TOHC sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread use of heavy metals in modern industries together with the sophisticated technological applications resulted in many serious threats that affect both humans and environment [1–3]. Among these heavy metals, mercury is one of the most dangerous and prevalent environmental toxins, with remarkable bioaccumulation and high toxic characteristics that caused major environmental and health problems [4,5]. In addition, mercury can be found in various forms including elemental, organic and inorganic materials. When human body absorbs traces of mercury, that leaked to water or foods especially sea foods, they accumulate inside the body and severely destroy the kidneys, the gastrointestinal tract, renal system, digestive system, neurological system, brain, and endocrine system [6–8]. Mercury also can be leached to water or soil through natural oceanic and volcanic emissions. Moreover, other sources of mercury contamination are the mercury-based industries such as chemicals, paints, agriculture, batteries, gold mining, dental amalgam, mercury vapor lamps, and electrical apparatus [9–11].

Therefore, designing an effective approach for the sensitive detection of tiny levels of mercury is critical and has received greet interest for environmental and health considerations. In dead, the amount of mercury can be determined using various traditional techniques such as atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) [12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [13], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [14], electrochemical methods and X-ray absorption spectroscopy [15]. However, the application of these methods suffers from drawbacks such as high cost, time-consuming, difficult to manage, and require complicated instruments [16]. Therefore, great attention has been directed toward the development of alternative detection strategies based on electrochemistry, spectrophotometry, chemosensors, and biosensors [17,18]. Due to their high sensitivity, fast reaction time, and flexible operation, fluorescent sensor approaches are promising means of detecting heavy metals pollutants [19–21]. Such methods involve a turn-on/off fluorescence sensor based on the intensity change of the fluorophore's initial emission upon interaction with the analyte, either a quenching or an enhancement [22]. Recently, numerous materials have been developed to serve as fluorescent sensors for detecting mercury II ions including carbon quantum dots [16,23–28], dyes [29–31], polymers [9,32], and other organic compounds [33–38].

In fact, the development of selective fluorescent probs for the detection of mercury II ions required compound characterized with high fluorescence quantum yield as well as contains function group that could coordinate with mercury. In this regard, compounds containing triazole-pyridine moieties are known to be excellent candidates that satisfy the production of fluorescent sensors due to their unique optical properties and high fluorescent quantum efficiency [39]. Their remarkable sensing ability comes from the presence of nitrogen function groups that coordinate with heavy metals especially mercury II ions forming stable complexes. These triazole-pyridine compounds are a conjugated system rich with electrons that allow them to act as a strong electron donor compound with unoccupied orbital acceptors [40]. Furthermore, triazole-pyridine compounds such as indolizines have been widely used as efficient fluorescent sensors for numerous analytes such as adamantanol, metal ions, phenols, or nitroaromatics [41].

In the present study we have prepared and characterized a novel fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg(II) ions based on triazole-pyridine derivative. We have also determined its optical properties and its selectivity toward mercury ions in different samples. Our investigation has been further extended to explore the quenching mechanism employing UV–vis spectroscopy. Later, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, that was successfully applied to similar systems [42,43], were conducted to obtain detailed structural and electronic details regarding the sensing mechanism.

Ethyl cyanoacetate, p- aminoacetophenone, acetylacetone, and sodium azide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Sodium nitrate, potassium carbonate, and ammonium acetate were brought from Loba chemie Co. Acetic acid, methanol, 1,4-dioxane, and acetone were received from fisher scientific Co. All metal salts including AlCl3, CaCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, FeSO4, HgCl2, KCl, MgSO4, NiCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CdCl2·H2O, Li2CO3, NaCl, PbCl2, SnCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, CuCl2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. and fisher scientific Co. All chemicals were AR grade and were used as received without any further purification.

1-(4-Azidophenyl) ethenone (AZPE) was synthesized according to the procedure described in the previous reports [44]. An ice-cold aqueous solution of sodium nitrate (21 mmol) was added dropwise to an aqueous solution of p- aminoacetophenone (20 mmol) in a dil. HCl at 0–5 °C and the resulting diazonium salt solution was stirred for 5 min, described in Scheme1 . A cooled aqueous solution of sodium azide (20 mmol) was added to the formed diazonium salt solution with continuous stirring until a yellow precipitate was produced. The resulting powder was filtered under suction to produce AZPE as pall yellow powder with yield (94 %) and m.p. (40–42 °C).

AMTP was synthesized as mentioned in a previous article [44] as shown in Scheme 1. Briefly, a mixture consisting of AZPE (15 mmol) and acetylacetone (15 mmol) was heated for 3 h at 70 °C in the presence of potassium carbonate (20 mmol). The reaction mixture was allowed to cool then filtered and poured over crushed ice followed by neutralized with HCl. The resulted precipitate was filtered out and recrystallized from H2O to provide pure product AMTP as a white powder with yield (46.5 %) and m.p. (120 – 122 °C).

The targeted TOHC was synthesized using solvent-free method through fusing cinnamaldehyde (15 mmol) with AMTP and ethyl cyanoacetate (30 mmol) in the presence of ammonium acetate for 10 min, Scheme1. The reaction mixture was triturated with ethanol then crystallized from ethanol and acetic acid mixture to yield TOHC as a yellow powder.

The detection of Hg2+ ions was performed at room temperature and a buffer solution with pH = 7 using TOHC as fluorophore. The sensitivity experiment test of TOHC toward Hg2+ was performed according to fluorescence titrimetric method [23,30,37]. Firstly, 500 ϻL of dioxane solution of TOHC (10−3 mol. L−1) and 1 ml of buffer solution (pH 7) were mixed, and then 500 ϻL of various concentration of Hg2+ were added to the solution mixture. The resulting solution containing Hg2+ concentration in the range from 0 to 75 µmol. L−1 were analyzed by fluorometer at an excitation wavelength equal to 424 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was performed by recording the change in relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The selectivity experiment test was confirmed using 500 ϻL of each different metal ion (10−3 mol. L−1) instead of Hg2+ in the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of mercury (II) ions in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the applicability of TOHC for detecting Hg2+ in real samples, five different water sources including (i) deionized water, (ii) tap water (TDS 189 mg/L, Chlorides 28 mg/L, Alkalinity 132 mg/L, total hardness 152 mg/L, calcium hardness 76 mg/L, magnesium hardness 76 mg/L, calcium 30.4 mg/L, magnesium 18.24 mg/L, permanent hardness 20 mg/L, aluminum 0.12 mg/L, phosphate 0.25 mg/L, silica 2.66 mg/L, cadmium 0.002 mg/L), (iii) Mediterranean water (calcium 450 mg/L, magnesium 1450 mg/L, sodium 12500 mg/L, potassium 450 mg/L, sulphate 3410 mg/L, bicarbonates 160 mg/L, chlorides 22100 mg/L), (iv) Nile River water (TDS 313 mg/L, Chlorides 29 mg/L, Alkalinity 146 mg/L, total hardness 166 mg/L, calcium hardness 104 mg/L, magnesium hardness 62 mg/L, calcium 41.6 mg/L, magnesium 14.88 mg/L, permanent hardness 20 mg/L, ammonia 0.33 mg/L, phosphate 0.825 mg/L, nitrite 0.016 mg/L, cadmium 0.002 mg/L), and (v) drinking water sample obtained from commercial sources (calcium 17.6 mg/L, magnesium 5.28 mg/L, sodium 17.6 mg/L, potassium 2.4 mg/L, bicarbonates 97.6 mg/L, sulphate 12.11 mg/L, chlorides 9 mg/L, silica 9 mg/L, TDS 120 mg/L) were used to prepare real Hg2+ samples. For each 1L water sample, HgCl2 (8.145–13.575 mg) was added to produce five different real samples containing 30–50 µM of Hg2+ ions. The concentration of Hg2+ in these samples was determined by standard colorimetric method using excess KI as coloring agent. In more details, 2 ml of each water sample was added to 5 ml of KI (2 M) and the resulting solution was diluted to a constant volume of 10 ml with distilled water. After that, the solution was analyzed using UV–vis spectrophotometer at wavelength 380 nm [45]. Also, the concentration of Hg2+ ions in each water sample was determined using TOHC as a fluorescence sensor as described in the present study and compared with the results of standard colorimetric method.

Spectroscopic data were collected using the following equipment: UV–visible spectra was recorded using Jasco V-630 spectrophotometer with a 1.0 cm quartz cell and wavelength ranges 200 – 800 nm. Fluorescence spectrum was performed using a Jasco FP-8350 spectrofluorometer with excitation wavelength 424 nm and emission wavelength range 300 – 800 nm. FTIR spectra was carry out using Jasco FTIR-4100 spectrophotometer, the sample was prepared as KBr discs, and the data was recorded in the range of 4000–––400 cm−1. 1H NMR analysis was performed using Bruker WP at 300 MHz with DMSO‑d 6 as solvent and TMS serving as the internal reference at 0–10 ppm. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a Shimadzu thermogravimetric analyzer at temperatures ranging from ambient temperature to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were captured using a Cu-K line (λ = 0.15406 nm) as the radiation source on a Siemens D-500 X-ray diffractometer. The measurements were scanned at a rate of 10°/min across an angular range of 2θ = 3°–80°.

To obtain mechanistic insights into the interaction between Hg(II) and the TOHC molecule, we used DFT and in particular B3LYP functional[46–48] as implemented in Gaussian16.[49] For all the atoms, 6-31G(d) basis set was used while Hg(II) has been treated using aug-cc-pV5Z-PP basis set. In fact, the combination of B3LYP functional and 6-31G(d) basis set has been successfully used for the treatment of various metal-containing chemical systems.[50–53] The initial model was built based on the information obtained from the experimental investigations which indicate that the ratio of the formed Hg(II)-chromophore complex is 1:2, meaning one metal ion bound to two TOHC chromophore.

Since our chromophore has the ability to exist in to enantiomers ketone and enol, we explored the sensing mechanism of the two possibilities and both of them binds in a 2:1 ratio to the Hg(II), Scheme 2 . We initially considered the ketone form of the TOHC which could bind to the Hg(II) only though one of their azo nitrogen atoms present in triazole moiety.

Alternatively, TOHC could also exist in its enol enantiomer in which the hydrogen atom of the –NH- group, located in pyridine ring, has shifted into the adjacent oxygen atom, a keto-enol tautomerism. This intramolecular hydrogen transfer led to obtaining unprotonated N center in the pyridine moiety of each ligand capable of coordinating to the Hg(II) ion. The remaining chelating atom is the N atom of the azo group from the triazole moiety in each of the two TOHC molecules. Overall, the two ligands are identically coordinated to the Hg(II) in a bidentate fashion resulting in a tetrahedral geometry where N atom is the coordinating atom, Scheme 2. The two possible complexes were fully characterized and then frequency calculation was performed on each geometry to further validate its identity as a stationary point.

The structure of all the prepared compounds including AZPE, AMTP, and TOHC was confirmed using both FTIR and 1H NMR analysis. Starting with FTIR analysis which is presented in Fig. 1
.a. the results demonstrate the following characteristic peaks for AZPT: IR (KBr, cm−1): 2095 (N3), 1671 (C = O), 3101 (C–H stretching aliphatic), 2995 (C–H stretching aromatic), 1593 (C–C benzene ring), 1275 (C-N stretching). Meanwhile, AMTP exhibits the following characteristic peaks: IR (KBr, cm−1): 1684 (C = O, acetyl), 1600 (N = N), 3064 (C–H stretching aliphatic), 2926 (C–H stretching aromatic), 1547 (C–C benzene ring), 1510 (C = C triazole), 1273 (C-N stretching). In addition, TOHC appears the following characteristic peaks: IR (KBr, cm−1): 3039 (C–H stretching aliphatic), 2927 (C–H stretching aromatic), 2215 (C≡N), 1717 (C = C, olefinic), 1685 (C = O), 1576 (N = N, Triazole), 1607 (C = C, aromatic).

Moving into 1H NMR analysis, 1H NMR analysis of AZPE is presented in Figure S1 in supplementary information and the results appear the following signals: 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO-D6); δ 2.49 (3H, s, COCH3), 7.23 (2H, d, Ar-H), 7.97(2H, d, Ar-H). While Figure S2 in supplementary information shows 1H NMR analysis of AMTP and the results are: 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO-D6); δ 2.55 (3H, s, COCH3-triazole), 2.63 (3H, s, CH3-triazole), 2.66 (3H, s, p-COCH3), 7.80 (2H, d, Ar-H), 8.18 (2H, d, Ar-H). Also 1H NMR analysis of TOHC is shown in Fig. 1.b and the results are: 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO-D6); δ 1.91 (3H, s, CH3-triazole), 7.26–7.33 (14H, M, Ar-H), 7.47–7.48 (4H, M, olefinic-H), 7.86 (2H, S, Ar-H), 8.19 (1H, s, NH), 8.37 (1H, s, NH).

The typical XRD patterns of TOHC is presented in Fig. 2
.a and only two broader diffraction peaks centered at 2θ equal to 22.81° and 42.54° have been noticed. This behavior of XRD patterns confirms the amorphous structure of TOHC.

The thermal properties of TOHC (fluorophore) were investigated using the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) technique and the results are presented in Fig. 2 .b. Both TGA and DTA plots for TOHC display a small reduction in sample weight starting from 332 K up to 415 K with a weight loss percentage equal to 2.74 %. Also, two main degradation stages in the temperature ranges 415–––593 K and 593 – 1073 K with weight loss percentage equal to 26.48 % and 30.76 %, respectively, have been observed. The small weight loss at 332 K could be attributed to evaporation of moisture or remained organic solvents after drying process whereas the degradation of the other two main stages (stage 2 and 3) could be attributed to the degradation of TOHC.

According to Coats-Redfern, the first order thermal degradation process can be determined by the following equation [54,55].
(1)
log
- log
1 - α
T
2
= log
A
′
R
θ
E
∗
1 -
2 R T
E
∗
-
E
∗
2.303 R T
where α is the fraction of sample decomposed at temperature T, A' is Arrhenius constant, R is general gas constant, θ is the heating rate and E* is the activation energy. The value of α is calculated from the experimental data according to the following equation
(2)
α =
W o
-
W t
W o
-
W f
where Wo is the initial weight of the sample, Wt is the weight of the sample at any given temperature and Wf is the final weight of the sample after completion of the reaction. The values of activation energy and Arrhenius constant are calculated after applying equation (1) on the experimental data of TGA (Figure S3 in supplementary information). Additionally, thermodynamic parameters of activation ΔS*, ΔH*, and ΔG* are calculated from equations (3) – (5) [55].
(3)
Δ
S
∗
= 2.303 R
log
A
′
h
K B
T
(4)
Δ
H
∗
=
E
∗
- R T
(5)
Δ
G
∗
= Δ
H
∗
- T Δ
S
∗
where KB, h are Boltzmann and Planck constants, respectively. All the results including thermal activation energy, Arrhenius constant and thermodynamic parameters for TOHC are summarized in Table 1 . These results exhibit that, both ΔG* and ΔH* have positive values which confirm the non-spontaneous and endothermic degradation process of TOHC.

Optical properties of TOHC were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in 1,4-dioxane as solvent and a wavelength range from 200 to 900 nm and the results were illustrated in Fig. 3 . Starting with absorption properties of TOHC, the results in (Fig. 3 .a) show two sharp absorption peaks appear at a wavelength equal to 271 and 337 nm. The absorption peak at 271 nm is due to π–π* electronic transition of double bonds of aromatic rings in TOHC. However, the absorption peak at 337 is due to n–π* electronic transition of lone pair electrons on oxygen and nitrogen functional groups in TOHC [56]. Moving into fluorescence properties of TOHC, the results in (Fig. 3b) appear that TOHC exhibits a maximum Photoluminescence (PL) emission at 466.6 nm when it excited at 424 nm.

Also, our study was extended to investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of the fluorophore (TOHC) at different pH value ranged from 1 to 9 using standard buffer solutions and the results are presented in (Fig. 4 ). It is noted from this figure that, raising the value of pH from 1 to 9 leads to obvious enhancement in fluorescence intensity of TOHC. This behavior could be attributed to deprotonation of pyridium unit on TOHC which will affect the electronic structures of fluorophore and this observation was in agreement with other reports [57,58].

The ability of TOHC to detect Hg2+ ions was performed using fluorescence titrimetric method. The influence of adding Hg2+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of TOHC were determined by recording the quenching of fluorescence intensity of TOHC after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ ions and the results are illustrated in Fig. 5
a. According to Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of TOHC is obviously quenched upon raising the concentration of Hg2+ ions from 0 to 75 µM. This behavior indicates that there is an interaction between TOHC and Hg2+ ions as well as confirms the validations of TOHC to be used as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ ions. Also, the dependence of concentration of Hg2+ ions as a function of fluorescence quenching of TOHC is determined by using the following Stern-Volmer equation [59,60].
(6)
F o
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ M ]
where F∘ is initial fluorescence intensity of TOHC before adding Hg2+ ions, F is the fluorescence intensity of TOHC after adding Hg2+ ions, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant, and M is the molar concentration of Hg2+ ions. Applying stern-Volmer equation to the experimental data obtained by adding different concentration of Hg2+ ions in the range of 0 – 75 µM produces a straight line as shown in Fig. 5 b. In addition, the excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) confirms the correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of TOHC. Furthermore, it provides an easy and accurate equation (
F o
F
= 1.22139 + 0.0224
[
Hg
2 +
]
) for determining the concentration of Hg2+ ions.

Moreover, our study is extended to assess the selectivity of TOHC toward Hg2+ ions. In order to carry out this purpose, 10−3 M of fifteen different cation solution including Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ are added to TOHC followed by measuring the fluorescence intensity before and after adding each metal cation and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . The results appear that all the fifteen metal cations have a little effect on the fluorescence intensity of TOHC and leads to small fluorescence quenching ranged from 7 % to 14.4 % of the initial fluorescence intensity of TOHC. While when Hg2+ ions are added, it causes high quenching for the fluorescence intensity of TOHC and the percentage reached up to 82 % when the same conditions of other fifteen metal cation are used. These results reflect and confirm the high selectivity of TOHC to interact with mercury (II) ions and recommends the potential applicability of TOHC to be used as a selective sensor for Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism between TOHC and Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The stoichiometry between the designed TOHC sensor and Hg2+ ions is estimated using a Job’s Plot method [35]. For this purpose, different volumes (0 – 1 ml) of Hg2+ ions (10−3 mol/L) are added to the corresponding volumes (1 – 0 ml) of TOHC (10−3 mol/L) and then the resulting solutions was diluted with 1 ml deionized water to produce different concentrations, in terms of mole fraction, of Hg2+ ions (in the range of 0 to 1). The fluorescent intensity of each solution mixture is measured by fluorometer, and the difference in emission intensity (F – Fo) is plotted against the mole fraction of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 7
.a). According to this figure, Job’s plot appears a maximum point corresponded to a mole fraction of Hg2+ ions equal to 0.33 which indicate that the stochiometric binding ratio between TOHC and Hg2+ ions is 2:1. This binding ratio indicate that Hg2+ ions is sandwiched between two molecules of TOHC [35].

Moreover, the present study extended to determine the quenching mechanism of TOHC by Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching process could potentially occur by three main mechanisms: energy transfer, charge diverting, or surface absorption [16] To determine the suitable mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of TOHC under the influence of Hg2+ ions, UV–vis spectra is recorded before and after adding of Hg2+ ions and the results are presented in Fig. 7b. The absorption peak of TOHC at wavelength 271 nm is blue shifted toward 250 nm while the absorption peak at wavelength 337 nm is red shifted to 344 nm when Hg2+ ions is added to the solution of TOHC. This behavior confirming the contribution of nitrogen atom of triazole and/or pyridine moieties in coordination of Hg2+ ions and forming metal complex [16,23,26,61–64]. In addition, it is reported that Hg2+ ions may allow a variety of coordination numbers, however four coordinate tetrahedral species is one of the most prevalent [65]. Furthermore, Hg2+ ions have the ability to interact with N-atoms which leads to increase intramolecular charge transfer between the donor and acceptor units of the sensor [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection analysis of mercury in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of TOHC fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions was assessed by using real water sample containing Hg2+ ions. Initially the concentration of Hg2+ ions in these real water samples was determined by the aid of standard colorimetric method using KI as coloring agent (see Figure S4 of calibration curve in supplementary information) [45]. After that, TOHC sensor is used to determine the concentration of Hg2+ in real water samples under consideration. Table 2 illustrates a comparison between the concentration of Hg2+ ions in real water samples obtained when the standard colorimetric method and TOHC senor are used, respectively. As seen from Table 2, TOHC probe is able to recover 102.27 %, 105.05 %, 86.55 %, 99.46 %, and 96.33 % of mercury from deionized water, tap water, and drinking water, Mediterranean water, Nile River water sample, respectively. These results confirms that TOHC can be served as an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.

The structure of the formed complex between the Hg(II) and two TOHC ligands has been successfully characterized using the approach discussed in the computational methods section above. We initially considered the ketone form of the TOHC which binds to the Hg(II) in a 2:1 ratio and thereby resulted in the ligation of the two ligands though one of their azo nitrogen atoms, Figure S5. Considering the enol possibility of the TOHC molecule, a thermodynamically more stable complex where the Hg(II)-ligand complex adopts a tetrahedral geometry has been characterized, Fig. 8 . The average coordination distance between the Hg(II) ion and the N atoms of TOHC was found to be approximately 2.28 Å indicating tight coordinating bonds.

Moreover, to obtain deeper understanding regardingthe impact of Hg2+ ligation on the electronic distribution over the fluorophores, we displayed the frontier molecular orbitals obtained from our optimization. Namely, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) were visualized over the associated atoms in Fig. 8. According to the visualized HOMO over Hg-complex, a clear electronic deficiency around the Hg2+ center has been noticed indicating the involvement of the N’s lone pair electrons of the fluorophore in the ligation to the metal ion. In fact, the principle reason for the fluorescence activity of the organic compound is the delocalization of electrons over the triazole-pyridine moieties. Without the presence of coordinated ligand, the HOMO of this flurophore would be localized over triazole-pyridine moieties but this has been diminished upon the ligation to Hg ions. In particular, the electron density over the coordinated regions to the Hg ions has been minimized, Fig. 8. This observation could possibly underline the reason for the observed quenching of the TOHC fluorescence upon Hg2+ chelation. Moreover, displaying the LUMO demonstrates the delocalization of the electrons near the Hg2+ion around triazole-pyridine moieties in contrast to our findings for the HOMO. This observation of the great difference between HOMO and LUMO aurrounding Hg ions in the literature could support our earlier hypothesis regarding the coordination of the lone pair electrons of the triazole-pyridine moieties to the empty orbitals of Hg2+.

In this study, we have successfully identified a novel fluorescent sensor based on triazole-pyridine derivative (TOHC) to determine the concentration of mercury II ions in polluted wastewater. This sensing strategy depends on nitrogen functional groups that are capable of selectively coordinating with Hg2+ as well as providing high fluorescent quantum efficiency. XRD analysis indicates that TOHC is amorphous, while TGA illustrates that, the sensor was thermally stable up to 415 K. Interestingly, our findings demonstrate that Hg2+could be effectively determined at quite low concentration up to 75 µM. In addition, the excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) of Stern-Volmer equation confirms the correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of TOHC. This correlation further provides an easy and accurate equation for determining the concentration of Hg2+ ions. Job’s plot methods illustrated that, mercury is sandwiched between two molecules of TOHC and the stochiometric binding ratio is 1:2. Furthermore, the blue and red shifts of the two characteristic absorption peaks of TOHC after adding mercury ions confirming the contribution of nitrogen atoms of triazole and/or pyridine moieties in coordination of Hg2+ ions and forming metal complex. DFT calculations were then performed to better identify the structure of the TOHC-Hg2+ complex and suggest a sensing pathway where Hg2+ adopts a tetrahedral geometry. In addition, TOHC probe can recover 102.27 %, 105.05 %, 86.55 %, 99.46 %, and 96.33 % of mercury from five real water samples including deionized water, tap water, and drinking water, Mediterranean water, and Nile River water sample, respectively. These results confirm that TOHC can be served as an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples which could be a valuable contribution to the global interest in affording a clean environment.
Funding
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Elsayed Elbayoumy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohamed Elhendawy: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mostafa M. Gaafar: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. EA Moawed: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Mohamed M. aboelnga: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the central laboratory at faculty of Science, Damietta university for providing the facilities to conduct UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2024.124589.
